1: wayyāśem hammeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš mas ʿal-hāʾāreṣ wəʾiyyê hayyām — 'King Ahasuerus imposed tribute (mas, H4522 = forced labor, levy, tax) on the land and the coastlands of the sea.' The concluding chapter opens with a note of Persian imperial administration: taxation. The term mas designates both the practice of forced labor (as in the Solomonic levy, 1 Kings 5:27) and tribute/taxation in kind. The 'coastlands of the sea' (ʾiyyê hayyām, H339 = islands, coastal regions) denotes the western territories of the empire — the Aegean coastlands of Asia Minor. The empire at maximum reach: tribute flows from its outermost territories to the Persian center.
2: wəḵol-maʿăśēh tāqpô ûgəḇûrāṯô ûpārāšaṯ gəḏûllaṯ Mordoḵay ʾăšer giddəlô hammeleḵ hălōʾ-hēm kəṯûḇîm ʿal-sēfer diḇrê hayyāmîm ləmalkê māḏay ûp̄āras — 'All the acts of his power and strength, and the account of the greatness of Mordecai to which the king advanced him, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia?' The standard regnal closure formula for Israelite kings (cf. 1 Kings 11:41; Esther 14:19,29 etc.) is applied here to the Persian king's reign — but the closure focuses not on the king's own deeds but on gəḏûllaṯ Mordoḵay (Mordecai's greatness). The official royal chronicles are invoked as verification of what the Esther scroll has narrated: the story is not private legend but publicly recorded history.
3: kî Mordoḵay hayyəhûḏî mišneh ləmeleḵ ʾAḥašwērôš wəgāḏôl layyəhûḏîm wərāṣûy lərōḇ ʾeḥāyw dōrēš ṭôḇ ləʿammô wədōḇēr šālôm ləḵol-zaraʿô — 'For Mordecai the Jew was second to King Ahasuerus, and great among the Jews, and accepted by the multitude of his brothers, seeking the welfare of his people and speaking peace to all his descendants.' The closing verse of Esther summarizes Mordecai's position in three concentric spheres: (1) the Persian court — mišneh ləmeleḵ (second to the king, H4932), exactly as Joseph was mišneh to Pharaoh (Genesis 41:43); (2) the Jewish community — gāḏôl layyəhûḏîm wərāṣûy lərōḇ ʾeḥāyw (great among the Jews and accepted by most of his brothers; rāṣāh, H7521 = to find pleasure in, accept, approve); (3) his own people's future — dōrēš ṭôḇ ləʿammô wədōḇēr šālôm ləḵol-zaraʿô (seeking good/welfare for his people and speaking peace to all his seed). The final word is zaraʿô (his seed/descendants) — the book closes with the future of the Jewish people secured, not just the present crisis resolved.
